title: Find Regex::find_iter
prompt: Find the function `find_iter` that returns an iterator over non-overlapping matches in the rust-lang/regex crate. There are multiple `find_iter` implementations across modules; report the one that lives on a `Regex` struct (the top-level public API), with file path, function name, a 3-5 line signature snippet, and one sentence on what it returns.

key_facts: method on the Regex struct (the top-level public API)
returns Matches iterator over non-overlapping matches
there are also find_iter on regex-automata Regex types (regex-automata/src/dfa/regex.rs:436, regex-automata/src/hybrid/regex.rs:399), those are lower-level

title: Trace pattern -> compiled Regex
prompt: When `Regex::new(pattern)` is called in the rust-lang/regex crate, trace the path from the user-facing entry point down to producing the matcher. Report the file + function of the top-level entry point, plus a 2-3 sentence trace through the key compilation steps (parsing → HIR → NFA or DFA construction → wrapping in Regex).

key_facts: Regex::new calls RegexBuilder or Builder::new -> build
Hir -> meta::Regex via meta::Builder::build_one (regex/src/builders.rs feeds meta layer)
meta layer in regex-automata/src/meta/ chooses strategy (literal/NFA/DFA)

title: Find call sites that select MatchKind::All
prompt: Find call sites in the regex codebase where `MatchKind::All` is selected or matched against (the leftmost-first/all-matches mode). Report 2-3 distinct call sites with file + line + a one-line snippet, plus a one-sentence explanation of what `MatchKind::All` means semantically.

ground_truth.purpose: MatchKind::All means the engine reports every overlapping match (typically used for finding all matches in find_iter, distinct from leftmost-first which prefers earliest+longest).

title: Compare PikeVM and BoundedBacktracker
prompt: Compare two NFA-based matchers in the rust-lang/regex crate: `PikeVM` and `BoundedBacktracker`. For each, find its primary source file + the type/struct name, and write a one-sentence description of how it executes a search. Then write a one-sentence comparison of when each is preferred.

A.does: Simulates the Thompson NFA in lockstep, tracking all possible states at each input position; guarantees O(m*n) time but doesn't support backreferences.

B.does: Standard backtracking NFA search bounded by a per-input-byte visited-set so it can't explode exponentially; supports capture groups efficiently when the haystack is small.

ground_truth.comparison: PikeVM is preferred for larger inputs where its O(m*n) bound matters; BoundedBacktracker is faster on small haystacks (under ~256 bytes by default) due to simpler control flow.
